B77 os pTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Corbin Mills, Amy en 1 Trillium Way MRN: [PHONE-5679], DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F Corbin KY 40701-8727 Adm: 11/5/2018, D/C: 11/6/2018

More Information

ERA RM oa RAGIN

Vaginal Hysterectomy, Care After

Refer to this sheet in the next few weeks. These instructions provide you with information about caring for yourself after your procedure. Your health care provider may also give you more specific instructions. Your treatment has been planned according to current medical practices, but problems sometimes occur. Call your health care provider if you have any problems or questions after your procedure.

What can I expect after the procedure?

After the procedure, it is common to have:

¢ Pain.

¢ Soreness and numbness in your incision areas. e Vaginal bleeding and discharge.

« Constipation.

¢ Temporary problems emptying the bladder.

e Feelings of sadness or other emotions.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

* Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider.

* Ifyou were prescribed an antibiotic medicine, take it as told by your health care provider. Do not stop taking the antibiotic even if you start to feel better.

¢ Do not drive or operate heavy machinery while taking prescription pain medicine.

Activity

¢ Return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider. Ask your health care provider what activities are safe for you.

¢ Get regular exercise as told by your health care provider. You may be told to take short walks every day and go farther each time.

¢ Do not lift anything that is heavier than 10 lb (4.5 kg).

General instructions

¢ Do not put anything in your vagina for 6 weeks after your surgery or as told by your health care provider. This includes tampons and douches.

¢ Do not have sex until your health care provider says you can.

¢ Do not take baths, swim, or use a hot tub until your health care provider approves.

¢ Drink enough fluid to keep your urine clear or pale yellow.

¢ Do not drive for 24 hours if you were given a sedative.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is 4 important. 7

Urethral Vaginal Sling, Care After

Refer to this sheet in the next few weeks. These instructions provide you with information on caring for yourself after your procedure. Your health care provider may also give you more specific instructions. Your treatment has been planned according to current medical practices, but problems sometimes occur. Call your health care provider if you have any problems or questions after your procedure.

What can [ expect after the procedure?

After your procedure, it is typical to have the following:

¢ Acatheter in your bladder until your bladder is able to work on its own properly. You will be instructed on how to empty the catheter bag. e Absorbable stitches in your incisions. They will slowly dissolve over 1-2 months.

Follow these instructions at home:

e Get plenty of rest.

* Only take over-the-counter or prescription medicines as directed by your health care provider. Do not take aspirin because it can cause bleeding.

¢ Do not take baths. Take showers until your health care provider tells you otherwise.

¢ You may resume your usual diet. Eat a well-balanced diet.

e Drink enough fluids to keep your urine clear or pale yellow.

¢ Limit exercise and activities as directed by your health care provider. Do not lift anything heavier than 5 pounds (2.3 kg),

¢ Do not douche, use tampons, or have sexual intercourse for 6 weeks after your procedure.

¢ Follow up with your health care provider as directed.

Contact a health care provider if:

e You have a heavy or bad smelling vaginal discharge. * You have a rash.

¢ You have pain that is not controlled with medicines. ¢ You have lightheadedness or feel faint.

Get help right away if:

¢ You have a fever.

¢« You have vaginal bleeding.

°¢ You faint.

¢ You have shortness of breath.

¢ You have chest, abdominal, or leg pain.

¢ You have pain when urinating or cannot urinate.

¢ Your catheter is still in your bladder and becomes blocked. ¢ You have swelling, redness, and pain in the vaginal area.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you Generated on 6/11/19 9:59 AM Page 141

BF on PTIST HEALTH Baptist Health Corbin Mills, Amy oes 4 Trillium Way MRN: [PHONE-5689], DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F Corbin KY 40701-8727 Adm: 11/5/2018, D/C: 11/6/2018

Ibuprofen tablets and capsules

What is this medicine?

IBUPROFEN (eye BYOO proe fen) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It is used for dental pain, fever, headaches or migraines, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or painful monthly periods. It can also relieve minor aches and pains caused by a cold, flu, or sore throat.

This medicine may be used for other purposes; ask your health care provider or pharmacist if you have questions.

COMMON BRAND NAME(S): Advil, Advil Junior Strength, Advil Migraine, Genpril, Ibren, IBU, Midol, Midol Cramps and Body Aches, Motrin, Motrin IB, Motrin Junior Strength, Motrin Migraine Pain, Samson-8, Toxicology Saliva Collection

What should I tell my health care provider before I take this medicine? They need to know if you have any of these conditions:

-asthma

-cigarette smoker

-drink more than 3 alcohol containing drinks a day

-heart disease or circulation problems such as heart failure or leg edema (fluid retention) -high blood pressure

-kidney disease

-liver disease

-stomach bleeding or ulcers

-an unusual or allergic reaction to ibuprofen, aspirin, other NSAIDS, other medicines, foods, dyes, or preservatives

-pregnant or trying to get pregnant -breast-feeding How should I use this medicine?

Take this medicine by mouth with a glass of water. Follow the directions on the prescription label. Take this medicine with food if your stomach gets upset. Try to not lie down for at least 10 minutes after you take the medicine. Take your medicine at regular intervals. Do not take your medicine more often than directed.

A special MedGuide will be given to you by the pharmacist with each prescription and refill. Be sure to read this information carefully each time.

Talk to your pediatrician regarding the use of this medicine in children. Special care may be needed. Generated on 6/11/19 9:59 AM Page 143




Sa BAPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Corbin Mills, Amy ae 1 Trillium Way MRN: [PHONE-5690], DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F Corbin KY 40701-8727 Adm: 11/5/2018, D/C: 11/6/2018

Overdosage: If you think you have taken too much of this medicine contact a poison control center or emergency room at once.

NOTE: This medicine is only for you. Do not share this medicine with others.

What if I miss a dose?

If you miss a dose, take it as soon as you can. If it is almost time for your next dose, take only that dose. Do not take double or extra doses.

What may interact with this medicine?

Do not take this medicine with any of the following medications: -cidofovir

-ketorolac

-methotrexate

-pemetrexed

This medicine may also interact with the following medications: -alcohol

-aspirin

-diuretics

-lithium

-other drugs for inflammation like prednisone

-warfarin

This list may not describe all possible interactions. Give your health care provider a list of all the medicines, herbs, non-prescription drugs, or dietary supplements you use. Also tell them if you smoke, drink alcohol, or use illegal drugs. Some items may interact with your medicine.

What should I watch for while using this medicine? Tell your doctor or healthcare professional if your symptoms do not start to get better or if they get worse.

This medicine does not prevent heart attack or stroke. In fact, this medicine may increase the chance of a heart attack or stroke. The chance may increase with longer use of this medicine and in people who have heart disease, If you take aspirin to prevent heart attack or stroke, talk with your doctor or health care professional.

Do not take other medicines that contain aspirin, ibuprofen, or naproxen with this medicine. Side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, or ulcers may be more likely to occur. Many medicines available without a prescription should not be taken with this medicine.

This medicine can cause ulcers and bleeding in the stomach and intestines at any time during treatment. Ulcers Generated on 6/11/19 9:59 AM Page 144




BF 5 pTisT HEALTH Baptist Health Corbin Mills, Amy oe 1 Trillium Way MRN: [PHONE-5691], DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F Corbin KY 40701-8727 Adm: 11/5/2018, D/C: 11/6/2018

and bleeding can happen without warning symptoms and can cause death. To reduce your risk, do not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol while you are taking this medicine.

You may get drowsy or dizzy. Do not drive, use machinery, or do anything that needs mental alertness until you know how this medicine affects you. Do not stand or sit up quickly, especially if you are an older patient. This reduces the risk of dizzy or fainting spells.

This medicine can cause you to bleed more easily. Try to avoid damage to your teeth and gums when you brush or floss your teeth,

This medicine may be used to treat migraines. If you take migraine medicines for 10 or more days a month, your migraines may get worse. Keep a diary of headache days and medicine use. Contact your healthcare professional if your migraine attacks occur more frequently.

What side effects may I notice from receiving this medicine?

Side effects that you should report to your doctor or health care professional as soon as possible: -allergic reactions like skin rash, itching or hives, swelling of the face, lips, or tongue

~severe stomach pain

-signs and symptoms of bleeding such as bloody or black, tarry stools; red or dark-brown urine; spitting up blood or brown material that looks like coffee grounds; red spots on the skin; unusual bruising or bleeding from the eye, gums, or nose

-signs and symptoms of a blood clot such as changes in vision; chest pain; severe, sudden headache; trouble speaking; sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm, or leg

-unexplained weight gain or swelling -unusually weak or tired -yellowing of eyes or skin

Side effects that usually do not require medical attention (report to your doctor or health care professional if they continue or are bothersome):

-bruising

-diarrhea

-dizziness, drowsiness -headache

-nausea, vomiting

This list may not describe all possible side effects. Call your doctor for medical advice about side effects. You may report side effects to FDA at 1-800-FDA-1088.

Where should I keep my medicine?

Docusate capsules

What is this medicine?

DOCUSATE (doc CUE sayt) is stool softener. It helps prevent constipation and straining or discomfort associated with hard or dry stools.

This medicine may be used for other purposes; ask your health care provider or pharmacist if you have questions.

COMMON BRAND NAME(S): Colace, Colace Clear, Correctol, D.O.S., DC, Doc-Q-Lace, DocuLace, Docusoft S, DOK, DOK Extra Strength, Dulcolax, Genasoft, Kao-Tin, Kaopectate Liqui-Gels, Phillips Stool Softener, Stool Softener, Stool Softner DC, Sulfolax, Sur-Q-Lax, Surfak, Uni-Ease

What should I tell my health care provider before I take this medicine? They need to know if you have any of these conditions:

~nausea or vomiting

-severe constipation

-stomach pain

-sudden change in bowel habit lasting more than 2 weeks

~an unusual or allergic reaction to docusate, other medicines, foods, dyes, or preservatives -pregnant or trying to get pregnant

~breast-feeding

How should I use this medicine?

Take this medicine by mouth with a glass of water. Follow the directions on the label. Take your doses at regular intervals. Do not take your medicine more often than directed.

Talk to your pediatrician regarding the use of this medicine in children. While this medicine may be prescribed for children as young as 2 years for selected conditions, precautions do apply.

Overdosage: If you think you have taken too much of this medicine contact a poison control center or emergency room at once.

NOTE: This medicine is only for you. Do not share this medicine with others. What if I miss a dose?

If you miss a dose, take it as soon as you can. If it is almost time for your next dose, take only that dose. Do not take double or extra doses.

What may interact with this medicine?

What should I watch for while using this medicine?

Do not use for more than one week without advice from your doctor or health care professional. If your constipation returns, check with your doctor or health care professional.

Drink plenty of water while taking this medicine. Drinking water helps decrease constipation.

Acetaminophen; Oxycodone tablets

What is this medicine?

ACETAMINOPHEN; OXYCODONE (a set a MEE noe fen; ox i KOE done) is a pain reliever. It is used to treat moderate to severe pain.

This medicine may be used for other purposes; ask your health care provider or pharmacist if you have questions.

COMMON BRAND NAME(S): Endocet, Magnacet, Narvox, Percocet, Perloxx, Primalev, Primlev, Roxicet, Xolox

What should I tell my health care provider before I take this medicine? They need to know if you have any of these conditions:

-~brain tumor

-Crohn's disease, inflammatory bowel disease, or ulcerative colitis -drug abuse or addiction

-head injury

-heart or circulation problems

-if you often drink alcohol

-kidney disease or problems going to the bathroom

-liver disease

-lung disease, asthma, or breathing problems

-an unusual or allergic reaction to acetaminophen, oxycodone, other opioid analgesics, other medicines, foods, dyes, or preservatives

-pregnant or trying to get pregnant -breast-feeding How should I use this medicine?

Take this medicine by mouth with a full glass of water. Follow the directions on the prescription label. You can take it with or without food. If it upsets your stomach, take it with food. Take your medicine at regular intervals. Do not take it more often than directed.

A special MedGuide will be given to you by the pharmacist with each prescription and refill. Be sure to read this information carefully each time.

Talk to your pediatrician regarding the use of this medicine in children. Special care may be needed.

wa BAPTIST HEALTH” Baptist Health Corbin Mills, Amy oe 1 Trillium Way MRN: [PHONE-5697], DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F Corbin KY 40701-8727 Adm: 11/5/2018, D/C: 11/6/2018

-medicines that relax muscles for surgery

This list may not describe all possible interactions. Give your health care provider a list of all the medicines, herbs, non-prescription drugs, or dietary supplements you use. Also tell them if you smoke, drink alcohol, or use illegal drugs. Some items may interact with your medicine.

What should I watch for while using this medicine?

Tell your doctor or health care professional if your pain does not go away, if it gets worse, or if you have new or a different type of pain. You may develop tolerance to the medicine. Tolerance means that you will need a higher dose of the medication for pain relief. Tolerance is normal and is expected if you take this medicine for a long time.

Do not suddenly stop taking your medicine because you may develop a severe reaction. Your body becomes used to the medicine. This does NOT mean you are addicted. Addiction is a behavior related to getting and using a drug for a non-medical reason. If you have pain, you have a medical reason to take pain medicine. Your doctor will tell you how much medicine to take. If your doctor wants you to stop the medicine, the dose will be slowly lowered over time to avoid any side effects.

There are different types of narcotic medicines (opiates). If you take more than one type at the same time or if you are taking another medicine that also causes drowsiness, you may have more side effects. Give your health care provider a list of all medicines you use. Your doctor will tell you how much medicine to take. Do not take more medicine than directed. Call emergency for help if you have problems breathing or unusual sleepiness.

Do not take other medicines that contain acetaminophen with this medicine. Always read labels carefully. If you have questions, ask your doctor or pharmacist.

If you take too much acetaminophen get medical help right away. Too much acetaminophen can be very dangerous and cause liver damage. Even if you do not have symptoms, it is important to get help right away.

You may get drowsy or dizzy. Do not drive, use machinery, or do anything that needs mental alertness until you know how this medicine affects you. Do not stand or sit up quickly, especially if you are an older patient. This reduces the risk of dizzy or fainting spells. Alcohol may interfere with the effect of this medicine. Avoid alcoholic drinks.

The medicine will cause constipation. Try to have a bowel movement at least every 2 to 3 days. If you do not have a bowel movement for 3 days, call your doctor or health care professional.

Your mouth may get dry. Chewing sugarless gum or sucking hard candy, and drinking plenty or water may help. Contact your doctor if the problem does not go away or is severe.

What side effects may I notice from receiving this medicine?

Side effects that you should report to your doctor or health care professional as soon as possible: -allergic reactions like skin rash, itching or hives, swelling of the face, lips, or tongue

-breathing problems

-confusion

-redness, blistering, peeling or loosening of the skin, including inside the mouth

-signs and symptoms of liver injury like dark yellow or brown urine; general ill feeling or flu-like symptoms; light-colored stools; loss of appetite; nausea; right upper belly pain; unusually weak or tired; yellowing of the eyes or skin

-signs and symptoms of low blood pressure like dizziness; feeling faint or lightheaded, falls; unusually weak or tired

-trouble passing urine or change in the amount of urine

Side effects that usually do not require medical attention (report to your doctor or health care professional if they continue or are bothersome):

-constipation

-dry mouth -nausea, vomiting -tiredness

This list may not describe all possible side effects. Call your doctor for medical advice about side effects. You may report side effects to FDA at 1-800-FDA-1088.

Where should I keep my medicine?

Keep out of the reach of children. This medicine can be abused. Keep your medicine in a safe place to protect it from theft. Do not share this medicine with anyone. Selling or giving away this medicine is dangerous and against the law.

This medicine may cause accidental overdose and death if it taken by other adults, children, or pets. Mix any unused medicine with a substance like cat litter or coffee grounds. Then throw the medicine away in a sealed container like a sealed bag or a coffee can with a lid. Do not use the medicine after the expiration date.

Store at room temperature between 20 and 25 degrees C (68 and 77 degrees F).

Hospital Course: Patient was admitted following elective C3-C4 ACDF by Dr. Shields and Dr. Johnson, which she tolerated the procedure well. she reported resolution of her preoperative neck and arm pain and felt her strength was improved postop. she had normal strength and sensation and neck was supple without hematoma. Patient was tolerating eating and ambulating without difficulty and was discharged home in stable condition.

Patient requested soft collar. She does not need collar post op for stability.

Consults:

IP CONSULT TO BRACE SHOP

Treatments: Surgery

Discharge Exam:

Mental Status: The patient is awake, alert, oriented to person, place, and time. There is no dysarthria. Recent and remote memory appears normal for age. Fund of knowledge appears normal for age.

Cranial Nerves: Pupils were equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally and consensually. EOMI without nystagmus. Visual fields were full to confrontation. Face was symmetric. There was intact light touch to V1, V2, V3. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Shoulder shrug was normal. Tongue protruded midline.

Motor: Tone was normal in all four extremities without fasciculations, atrophy, or myoclonus. There were no abnormal movements. Strength was 5/5 in bilateral hand intrinsics, bicep, triceps, deltoid. Strength is 5/5 in bilateral iliopsoas, quadriceps, anterior tibialis, extensor hallucis longer, and gastrocnemius.

Reflexes: The reflexes are 2/4 for biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar and achillies tendon bilaterally and symmetrically.

Sensory: Intact to light touch throughout all dermatones.

Cerebellar: There was no dysmetria. Fine motor coordination was intact.

Gait: Gait was normal based without ataxia. Tandem, heel, and toe gait were intact.

Incision: clean and dry no redness or swelling

Disposition: Home or Self Care

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: The patient presents for the planned procedure today. She was last seen by Dr. Shields on 12/19/19:

This 35 yr/o female has a history of neck pain. She states that these symptoms began after a fall on 4/26/19 in which she slipped on a wet floor at a bank. She fell forward against a bench and hit the back of her head and her

left shoulder. Immediately afterwards, she began to experience pain on the left side of her neck. In her last appointment, she also noted left arm pain extending from the upper arm to the mid forearm, and numbness in the fingers of the left hand, which also began after the fall. Patient notes weakness in the left arm and hand, especially on writing and typing. She is left handed. Strength in the right arm is normal. She has undergone 3 sessions of physical therapy to the left shoulder, from which she notes no relief of her pain. She notes some difficulties with balance. since her fall.

PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion at C3-4 with insertion of a 6 mm PEEK cage filled with VIA graft and bone chips.

PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Cervical disc osteophyte complex at C3-4.

POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Cervical disc osteophyte complex at C3-4. ASSISTANT: Tina Woodson.

DESCRIPTION OF PROCEDURE: The patient was placed in the supine position with the neck slightly hyperextended. The anterior aspect of the neck was shaved, prepped and draped in the usual fashion. An incision was outlined in the

upper aspect of the cervical spine on the right side. A cross-table cervical

OP Notes (continued

UNIT/AGE/GENDER: N.PERIOP I 36 F PATIENT NAME/DOB: MILLS, AMY JO [DOB-6556] UNIT NUMBER: EP02256299

ACCOUNT NUMBER: 10080087152

DATE OF OPERATION (S) /PROCEDURE(S): [DOB-5785]

SURGEON: John Johnson, MD

PROCEDURE PERFORMED: Anterior Anodyne cervical stabilizing plate, C3-C4.

PREOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Herniated nucleus pulposus, C3 to C4.

POSTOPERATIVE DIAGNOSIS: Herniated nucleus pulposus, C3 to C4.

FIRST ASSISTANT: Kristi Turner, APRN Kristi was present during the instrumentation and closure of the wound. PROCEDURE: After the patient had undergone a C3-4 discectomy and bone

grafting by Dr. Christopher Shields, which will be dictated as a separate procedure, the procedure was then turned over to the orthopedic spine team.

Using a caliper, a 13 mm in length Anodyne cervical stabilizing plate was sized to fit across from C3 to C4 and was transfixed to the vertebral body with 12 mm self-tapping screws, 2 into the vertebral body of C3 and 2 into the vertebral body of C4. All screws at the time of surgery had firm fixation by fill in bone. Lateral x-ray was taken to verify position of the plate, bone graft, and screws. One screw appeared to be slightly lower on the lateral at C3 level; however, it still appeared to be above the marker of the cage at the superior portion of C3. Because of the fact that it had good fixation in bone by fill, 2 x-rays were taken. Still, the position seemed to be a little bit inferior on the x-rays, but the cervical spine appeared to be somewhat oblique, and it was felt, because of the good fixation, that we would accept this. The wound was then closed in layers, sterile dressing was applied, and the patient was taken to the recovery room for further postoperative care.

John Johnson, MD

INSTRUCTIONS FOR YOUR SURGERY:

*Bathe with Chlorhexide (CHG) as directed on your Bathing Instruction Sheet or as instructed by your Surgeon *Call your Doctor if any changes in your health occur such as fever, flu or sores on your skin

*Do not eat or drink anything after midnight--UNLESS OTHERWISE DIRECTED BY YOUR SURGEON *No makeup, perfume, cologne or contacts---REMOVE ALL JEWELRY AND BODY PIERCINGS*

*You may brush your teeth

*Arrive day and time as directed by your Doctor

*Leave your valuables at home

“Wear loose, comfortable clothing

*Leave luggage in car

MEDICATION INSTRUCTIONS PER ANESTHESIA GUIDELINES: ONLY TAKE these STOP these medications 2 medications the morning of | weeks prior to surgery: surgery WITH SIP OF

WATER You may have a Norco pill if No Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen, needed Motrin, diclofenac

CONTINUE ALL OTHER MEDICATIONS NOT LISTED ABOVE UNTIL THE NIGHT BEFORE SURGERY

*IF YOU ARE CURRENTLY ON A BLOOD THINNER, ANTIPLATELET, AND/OR ANTI-COAGULANT, YOU MUST CONTACT THE PRESCRIBING DOCTOR AND YOUR SURGEON FOR INSTRUCTIONS ON WHEN TO STOP THE MEDICATIONS PRIOR TO SURGERY*

*A PRESCRIPTION FOR MUPIROCIN/BACTROBAN WILL BE SENT TO YOUR PHARMACY AND WILL BE AVAILABLE IN A FEW DAYS. PLEASE PICK UP AND USE AS DIRECTED--IF YOU HAVE PROBLEMS OBTAINING THIS MEDICINE, PLEASE CALL US AT 502- 629-7709

INSTRUCTIONS FOR YOUR SURGERY:

*Bathe with Chlorhexide (CHG) as directed on your Bathing Instruction Sheet or as instructed by your Surgeon *Call your Doctor if any changes in your health occur such as fever, flu or sores on your skin

*Do not eat or drink anything after midnight--UNLESS OTHERWISE DIRECTED BY YOUR SURGEON *No makeup, perfume, cologne or contacts---REMOVE ALL JEWELRY AND BODY PIERCINGS*

*You may brush your teeth

*Arrive day and time as directed by your Doctor

*Leave your valuables at home

“Wear loose, comfortable clothing

*Leave luggage in car

“You must have someone to drive you home

*Bring your picture ID, insurance card, co-pay if needed and any papers your doctor may have told you to bring the day of surgery

PLEASE CALL PREADMISSION TESTING AT 502- 629-7709 IF ANY QUESTIONS.

Go to Norton Hospital at 200 East Chestnut Street. The Garage may be accessed from either Chestnut Street or Gray Street. The Hospital may be accessed from the Garage on the 2nd floor. Come into the Hospital and Registration is on the Second floor by the Information desk. The Registration desk clerk will direct you to surgery on the lower level. Call 502- 629-7215 the day of your surgery if your are delayed or ill. Valet parking is available at the Chestnut Street entrance if you desire.

Acknowledged: Parker, Marilyn, RN 01/21/20 1023 for Placing Order

Diagnoses

Herniation of cervical intervertebral disc with radiculopathy [M50.10]

Order comments: The following discharge instructions were discussed with the patient and they verbalized understanding When you go home you may get up and be active You may go up and down stairs, but make sure to hold on to the rail and have someone with you You can ride in a car, but cannot drive until you have been cleared by your surgeon You can take the dressing off of your incision on Friday and may begin showering on Saturday. Check your incision daily. Notify our office if you notice redness, swelling or drainage from your incision or if you have fever over 100.5 Keep your head elevated on 4-5 pillows for at least a week-DO NOT LAY FLAT!!! Stick with soft foods that are easy to swallow. Take small bites and chew your food well. Ice and popsicles help with sore throat You may not lift anything over 5 pounds until cleared by your surgeon and do not do any kind of work over your head Absolutely no nicotine products Absolutely no Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen or any other kind of anti-inflammatory You cannot take Tylenol

her Orders (group 3 of 3) (continued

with your pain medicine. Your pain medicine has Tylenol in it. You cannot take both One of the side effects of the pain medication is constipation. You will probably need to take an over the counter laxative in addition to your stool softner. Please let our office know if you are having problems having a bowel movement One of the side effects of your muscle relaxer is drowsiness. If you begin to feel too drowsy that you cannot get up and safely ambulate, decrease the frequency of your muscle relaxer. Call our office at [PHONE-5916] if you have any problems. There is someone on-call 24 hours a day Follow up appointment with neurosurgery on 2/20/20 at 11:40 am with Stephanie Tichenor Nurse Practitioner.

Indications Herniation of cervical intervertebral disc with radiculopathy [M50.10 (ICD-10-CM)]

Problem: Hemodynamic Status

Dates: Start: 01/21/20 Description: Disciplines: Interdisciplinary Goal: Patient's vitals signs are stable Dates: Start: 01/21/20 Expected End: 01/24/20 Description: Assess and monitor patient's heart rate, rhythm, respiratory rate, peripheral pulses, capillary refill,

color, body temperature, intake and output, labs and physical activity tolerance. Observe for signs of chest pain (note location, duration, severity, radiation and associated symptoms such as diaphoresis, nausea, indigestion). Monitor for signs and symptoms of heart failure (eg. shortness of breath, edema of feet/ankles/legs, rapid irregular heart rate, coughing, wheezing, white/pink blood tinged sputum, sudden weight gain, chest pain). Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions as ordered.

Disciplines: Interdisciplinary Outcomes: Date/Time User Outcome 01/21/20 0115 Williams, Floremie, RN Progressing

Dates: Start: 01/21/20 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Remains infection free (Resolved) Dates: Start: 01/21/20 Expected End: 01/24/20 End: 01/21/20 Description: Assess and monitor vital signs, skin (color, moisture, integrity, turgor), respiratory status, urinary

and gastrointestinal status, and labs (WBC, cultures). Administer antibiotics and antipyretics as ordered. Ensure aseptic care of all intravenous lines, invasive tubes/drains and wounds. Monitor for signs and symptoms of infection (redness, warmth, discharge, increased body temperature). Wash hands properly before and after each patient care activity. Follow isolation guidelines per hospital protocol/policy. Collaborate with interdisciplinary team and initiate plan and interventions

as ordered. Disciplines: Interdisciplinary Outcomes: Date/Time User Outcome 01/21/20 1035 Baxter, Kylie B, PTA Completed 01/21/20 0115 Williams, Floremie, RN Progressing

1045 Comment: Discharge instructions given

Point: Post-Op Pneumonia Prevention (Done)

Description: Explain to patient/family/caregiver that people are at risk for pneumonia when they lay around more than usual. Lack of

exercise and mobility also increases the risk for pneumonia. Instruct the patient to walk every 2 hours for short distances (if unable to walk, they should sit on bedside every 2 hours and

dangle legs unless contraindicated).

1045 Comment: Discharge instructions given

Topic: Disease Management - PostOp General Surgery (Done)

Point: Edema Management (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver that patient may get puffy and look swollen. Need to reposition frequently, elevate

extremities above heart level if possible, use ice packs as ordered, maintain fluid and diet control.

Point: Incision/Wound Care (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for incision/wound to include: how to clean, when to clean and any special instruction for cleaning.

Know how to care for your wound. Emphasize good hand hygiene before and after caring for the the wound.

Teach about signs and symptoms of infection and when to notify healthcare provider.

Teach to tell family and friends to never touch wound or bandage and to clean their hands before and after visiting.

Instruct to keep incision/wound as clean and dry as possible.

Caution against: scrubbing or rubbing the incision, removing the tape strips, using lotion or powder around the incision,

exposing the incision to sunlight, taking a bath unless the incision can be kept dry.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Bandage/Dressing Care (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for bandage/dressing to include: how to change, when to change and any special instruction for changing. Demonstrate changing a bandage/dressing and receive a return demonstation. Teach to keep bandage/dressing clean and dry as possible. Caution against: using lots of tape, using lotion or powder around bandage, getting bandage/dressing wet when taking a bath or shower. Emphasize good hand hygiene. If patient is not to change bandage/dressing, instruct him/her to make follow-up appointment with healthcare provider to have bandage/dressing changed. Encourage the patient/family/caregiver to ask any questions about bandage/dressing care.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Description: Demonstrate to patient/family/caregiver how to support the incision while coughing and moving around.

Point: Falls Precautions (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver to keep call light in reach, bed in low position, wheels locked, to ask for help to get up, and to use non-slip socks when up.

* Instruct to call for help before getting out of bed

* Instruct to wear non-slip footwear when out of bed

* Instruct why they are at risk for falling

* Communicate "at risk" status with patient and family

* If discharging home, instruct on home modifications to reduce falls in the home

Point: Explain Pain Rating Scale (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on the appropriate pain rating scale. Encourage patient/family/caregiver to monitor patient's pain.

Patient Friendly Description: -Your nurse will ask you to rate our pain on a scale of zero (0), for no pain, to ten (10), the worst pain you can experience.

-Your nurse will ask you to set a pain goal within zero (0) to ten (10) so that he or she can be sure that what we are doing is working.

-Mild pain is 1-3, Moderate pain is 4-6 and severe pain is 7-10. -We may not be able to take away ALL pain, so try to identify a point that pain would be manageable.

-So if your pain goal is 4 or less and you are rating your pain at 7, your nurse would give you a pain medication and then re- check your pain to ensure it is moving toward your pain goal of 4.

Point: Positioning/Pressure Relief (Done) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on positioning and pressure relief. When in bed, teach that it is important to reposition at least every 2 hours. Point: Skin care (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver that skin should be inspected at least once daily. Use creams or oils on skin to maintain adequate skin moisture content. Avoid cold or dry air. Avoid temperature extremes. Prevent skin breakdown that could lead to bed sores. Have heels float in the air over edge of pillow.

Topic: Infection Prevention (In Progress)

Point: Multi-drug Resistant Organism (Done) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver about healthcare associate infection prevention strategies. Teach hand hygiene and wearing of gloves, gowns and mask when appropriate.

Explain that an MDRO is a strain of bacteria that is resistant to common antibiotics used to treat infections. Infections can vary, depending on the organism. MDROs can cause skin infections (boils, abscesses), urinary tract infections, blood stream infections, and pneumonia, and they can infect wounds, the respiratory tract and surgical sites.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Protect Others from Infection (Done)

Description:

Educate on respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette. To protect from further infection and to protect others from getting an infection, patient should cover nose and mouth with tissues when coughing. Encourage patient to place used tissues in a plastic bag that will not allow secretions to soak through. Patient should always wash their hands after using or handling used tissues.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Protect Yourself from Further Infection (Done)

Description: Educate that because patient is in a weakened condition he/she needs to avoid others who are sick as to not acquire another illness; should wear gloves, gown and face shield/eye protection.

Point: Demonstrate Proper Hand Hygiene (Done) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to perform proper hand hygiene. Explain that hand hygiene is the single most important step in preventing the spread of germs. Have them demonstrate proper hand washing technique using soap, water, friction, clean nails under running water, and dry hands on clean towel without touching dirty surfaces and using alcohol based decontaminates.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230 Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by FW at 1/20/2020 2230

Point: Falls Precautions (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver to keep call light in reach, bed in low position, wheels locked, to ask for help to get up, and to use non-slip socks when up.

* Instruct to call for help before getting out of bed

* Instruct to wear non-slip footwear when out of bed

* Instruct why they are at risk for falling

* Communicate "at risk" status with patient and family

* If discharging home, instruct on home modifications to reduce falls in the home

Point: Blood Transfusion (Done)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on Blood Transfusions and transfusion reactions. In a blood transfusion, donated blood is added to patient's own blood. A blood transfusion may also be done to supplement

various components of the patient's blood with donated blood products.

During a typical blood transfusion, certain parts of blood are delivered through an intravenous (IV) line that's placed in the patient. A blood transfusion usually takes two to four hours, though in an emergency it can be done much faster.

A blood transfusion boosts blood levels that are low, either because the body isn't making enough or because blood has been lost during surgery, injury or disease

AFTER VISIT SUMMARY

Amy J. Mills Men: £P02256299 CSN: 10080087152 [#] Disc disease of the neck ] 1/20/2020 - 1/21/2020 @ NORTON HOSPITAL Q NH 4K @, [PHONE-6042]

Instructions

Other instructions

Discharge instructions (specify) The following discharge instructions were discussed with the patient and they verbalized understanding When you go home you may get up and be active You may go up and down stairs, but make sure to hold on to the rail and have someone with you You can ride in a car, but cannot drive until you have been cleared by your surgeon You can take the dressing off of your incision on Friday and may begin showering on Saturday. Check your incision daily. Notify our office if you notice redness, swelling or drainage from your incision or if you have fever over 100.5 Keep your head elevated on 4-5 pillows for at least a week- DO NOT LAY FLAT!!! Stick with soft foods that are easy to swallow. Take small bites and chew your food well. Ice and popsicles help with sore throat You may not lift anything over 5 pounds until cleared by your surgeon and do not do any kind of work over your head Absolutely no nicotine products Absolutely no Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen or any other kind of anti-inflammatory You cannot take Tylenol with your pain medicine. Your pain medicine has Tylenol in it. You cannot take both One of the side effects of the pain medication is constipation. You will probably need to take an over the counter laxative in addition to your stool softner. Please let our office know if you are having problems having a bowel movement One of the side effects of your muscle relaxer is drowsiness. If you begin to feel too drowsy that you cannot get up and safely ambulate, decrease the frequency of your muscle relaxer. Call our office at [PHONE-6043] if you have any problems. There is someone on-call 24 hours a day Follow up appointment with neurosurgery on 2/20/20 at 11:40 am with Stephanie Tichenor Nurse Practitioner.

Medication Information (continued)

+ Remember to take your pain medication before activity and at bedtime. Your provider may advise you to take your pain medication at regular intervals (such as every 4 to 6 hours).

Be sure to get enough rest. If you are having trouble sleeping, talk to your provider.

Use pillows to support you when you sleep and when you do your coughing and deep breathing exercises Try using the alternative methods such as heating pads or cold therapy, walking, guided imagery tapes, listening to soft music, changing your position in bed, aromatherapy, and massage can help relieve your pain.

Safe medication storage and disposal Medications should be stored by patients in secure locations and excess pills should be disposed of properly, to minimize the risk of diversion and inappropriate use. To find a prescription drug disposal location near you please

visit https://odcp.ky.gov

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Norton Care Call

If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call

* Within 48 hours of discharge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-6052] (Caller ID will read Norton Care Call)

* You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone keypad.

* Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.

Instructions

Other instructions

Discharge instructions (specify) The following discharge instructions were discussed with the patient and they verbalized understanding When you go home you may get up and be active You may go up and down stairs, but make sure to hold on to the rail and have someone with you You can ride in a car, but cannot drive until you have been cleared by your surgeon You can take the dressing off of your incision on Friday and may begin showering on Saturday. Check your incision daily. Notify our office if you notice redness, swelling or drainage from your incision or if you have fever over 100.5 Keep your head elevated on 4-5 pillows for at least a week- DO NOT LAY FLAT!!! Stick with soft foods that are easy to swallow. Take small bites and chew your food well. Ice and popsicles help with sore throat You may not lift anything over 5 pounds until cleared by your surgeon and do not do any kind of work over your head Absolutely no nicotine products Absolutely no Advil, Aleve, Ibuprofen or any other kind of anti-inflammatory You cannot take Tylenol with your pain medicine. Your pain medicine has Tylenol in it. You cannot take both One of the side effects of the pain medication is constipation. You will probably need to take an over the counter laxative in addition to your stool softner. Please let our office know if you are having problems having a bowel movement One of the side effects of your muscle relaxer is drowsiness. If you begin to feel too drowsy that you cannot get up and safely ambulate, decrease the frequency of your muscle relaxer. Call our office at [PHONE-6056] if you have any problems. There is someone on-call 24 hours a day Follow up appointment with neurosurgery on 2/20/20 at 11:40 am with Stephanie Tichenor Nurse Practitioner.

Medication Information Preparation for Your Discharge: Your providers may have already given you your prescription for pain medication prior to your discharge date. If this is the case, it is best to be prepared and have your medication filled and ready for your return home from the hospital. You may want to have your pain pills with you on your ride home if you are traveling a long distance. While at Home: + Use medication only as directed. * Remember medication takes time to work. Most oral pain relievers need at least 20 minutes to take effect.

Khalily, Cyna, MD at 8/10/2020 1:35 PM Author: Khalily, Cyna, MD Service: — Author Type: Physician

OPERATIVE/PROCEDURE REPORT

Facility: Norton Pavilion Hospital

Patient Identification:

Name: Amy Mills

Age: 36 yr/o

Sex: female

DOB: [DOB-6556]

Medical Record Number: EP02256299 Account Number: 10091617656

Date of Operation/Procedure: 8/10/2020 Pre-Operative Diagnosis: left shoulder SLAP Post-Operative Diagnosis: Same

Procedure Performed: left shoulder arthroscopic #1 anterior labrum repair #2 acromioplasty

Surgeon: Cyna Khalily, MD

Anesthesia: General with supplemental regional Preoperative Antibiotics: Kefzol Complications: None

Drains: none

Specimens: None

Estimated blood loss: 5 mL

Description of Procedure: Patient was met in the preoperative area the operative extremity was identified and marked. They were then brought to the

Generated on 9/24/21 1:17 PM Page 286




RTO NORTON HOSPITAL Mills, Amy IN NORTON 200 E Chestnut Street MRN: EP02256299, DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F

Real people. Remarkable care Louisville KY 40202-1800 Acct #: 203419394 ‘ i Adm: 8/10/2020, D/C: 8/10/2020

[DOB-6128] - Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV Periop Services (continued)

OP Notes (continued

operative suite. After the satisfactory induction of general anesthesia,the operative extremity was prepped and draped in a normal sterile manner. Surgical time-out was then called to confirm that the patient had been given appropriate preoperative antibiotics, that this was the correct patient, correct procedure, and correct operative extremity.

A posterior portal was established in the glenohumeral joint was entered. An anterior portal was established.

The underside of the rotator cuff is intact. The articular cartilage of the glenohumeral joint was intact. As predicted on the MRI there was a discrete anterior attachment of the labrum at the 9 o'clock position. The bicep tendon and anchor were intact. An arthroscopic Freer was used to dissect the area of the tear and then a rasp was used to abrade the anterior bony glenoid.

The labrum was then reapproximated using a single bioabsorbable suture anchor.

An accessory superior lateral portal was established and scope was placed in the subacromial space. A complete subacromial bursectomy was performed and then the shaver was used to remove the underlying acromial spur. The top side of the rotator cuff was intact

All fluid and soft tissue debris was drained from the joint. The portals were closed with nylon, and covered with a dry sterile dressing.

The patient tolerated the procedure well and was returned to the recovery room

in satisfactory condition

8/10/2020 1:35 PM Cyna Khalily, MD

Procedure Orders

1. Interscalene Nerve Block [414888775] ordered by Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD

Procedure: Interscalene Nerve Block

Reason for Block/Indications: at surgeon's request and post-op pain management The procedure and alternatives were discussed as well as all clinically relevant risks and benefits. All questions were answered. The patient (and/or proxy) agreed to proceed, informed consent obtained

Sedation: IV sedation, See MAR for details

(NH 113-149 sec) (AUD,NWC,NBH, 123-172 sec) (Pediatric 89-169 sec)Laterality: Left Procedure timeout performed

Procedure site has been marked and confirmed by Anesthesia Provider

Patient position: Sitting Prep: Chlorhexidine.

Procedure Technique: Ultrasound guided. Ultrasound used to visualize needle placement in proximity to nerve being blocked.

Needle type: Stimuplex 10 G 20 cm

Start time: 8/10/2020 11:46 AM End time: 8/10/2020 11:47 AM Injection made incrementally with aspirations every 5 mL.

Additional Notes: Patient was identified, consent checked, and timeout performed. Patient was interviewed and risks and benefits of anesthesia and peripheral nerve block were discussed prior to procedure, and patient was in agreement. In addition to the description above, the procedure was performed as follows:

1) Under ultrasound guidance, a 20 gauge needle was inserted and placed in close proximity to the brachial plexus at the interscalene level.

2) Ultrasound was also used to visualize the spread of anesthetic in close proximity to the nerve being blocked. 3) The nerve appeared anatomically normal.

1. Interscalene Nerve Block [414888775] ordered by Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD

Procedure: Interscalene Nerve Block

Reason for Block/Indications: at surgeon's request and post-op pain management

The procedure and alternatives were discussed as well as all clinically relevant risks and benefits. All questions were answered. The patient (and/or proxy) agreed to proceed, informed consent obtained

Sedation: IV sedation, See MAR for details

RTO NORTON HOSPITAL Mills, Amy IN NORTON 200 E Chestnut Street MRN: EP02256299, DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F

Louisville KY 40202-1800 Acct #: 203419394 Adm: 8/10/2020, D/C: 8/10/2020 [DOB-6144] - Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV Periop Services (continued)

Anesthesia on 08/10/20 (continued

Procedure Technique: Ultrasound guided. Ultrasound used to visualize needle placement in proximity to nerve being blocked.

Real people. Remarkable care.

Needle type: Stimuplex 10 G 20 cm

Start time: 8/10/2020 11:46 AM End time: 8/10/2020 11:47 AM Injection made incrementally with aspirations every 5 mL.

Additional Notes: Patient was identified, consent checked, and timeout performed. Patient was interviewed and risks and benefits of anesthesia and peripheral nerve block were discussed prior to procedure, and patient was in agreement. In addition to the description above, the procedure was performed as follows:

1) Under ultrasound guidance, a 20 gauge needle was inserted and placed in close proximity to the brachial plexus at the interscalene level.

2) Ultrasound was also used to visualize the spread of anesthetic in close proximity to the nerve being blocked. 3) The nerve appeared anatomically normal.

4) There were no apparent abnormal pathological findings.

5) A permanent ultrasound image was saved to the patient's record.

There were no apparent complications, and patient was conversant throughout procedure. Patient tolerated procedure well. Please see MAR for drug dosages and times.

Interscalene Nerve Block

Ordering provider: Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD 08/10/20 1150 Order Filed by: Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD 08/10/20 1151

Narrative:

Noble, Ryan Matthew, MD 8/10/2020 11:51 AM

Procedure: Interscalene Nerve Block

Reason for Block/Indications: at surgeon's request and post-op pain management

The procedure and alternatives were discussed as well as all clinically relevant risks and benefits. All questions were answered. The patient (and/or proxy) agreed to proceed, informed consent obtained

Sedation: IV sedation, See MAR for details

(NH 113-149 sec) (AUD,NWC,NBH, 123-172 sec) (Pediatric 89-169 sec)Laterality: Left

Procedure timeout performed

Procedure site has been marked and confirmed by Anesthesia Provider

Patient position: Sitting Prep: Chlorhexidine.

Procedure Technique: Ultrasound guided. Ultrasound used to visualize needle placement in proximity to nerve being blocked.

Needle type: Stimuplex 10 G 20 cm

Generated on 9/24/21 1:17 PM

Resulted: 08/10/20 1150, Result status: Final result status: Completed

Page 317




NORTON HOSPITAL Mills, Amy IN NORTON 200 E Chestnut Street MRN: EP02256299, DOB: [DOB-6556], Sex: F

Louisville KY 40202-1800 Acct #: 203419394 Adm: 8/10/2020, D/C: 8/10/2020 [DOB-6160] - Admission (Discharged) in NH PAV Periop Services (continued)

Anesthesia on 08/10/20 (continued

Real people. Remarkable care.

Start time: 8/10/2020 11:46 AM End time: 8/10/2020 11:47 AM Injection made incrementally with aspirations every 5 mL.

Additional Notes: Patient was identified, consent checked, and timeout performed. Patient was interviewed and risks and benefits of anesthesia and peripheral nerve block were discussed prior to procedure, and patient was in agreement. In addition to the description above, the procedure was performed as follows:

1) Under ultrasound guidance, a 20 gauge needle was inserted and placed in close proximity to the brachial plexus at the interscalene level.

2) Ultrasound was also used to visualize the spread of anesthetic in close proximity to the nerve being blocked.

3) The nerve appeared anatomically normal.

4) There were no apparent abnormal pathological findings.

5) A permanent ultrasound image was saved to the patient's record. There were no apparent complications, and patient was conversant

throughout procedure. Patient tolerated procedure well. Please see MAR for drug dosages and times.

Provide home instruction sheet (specify) (Active) Electronically signed by: Khalily, Cyna, MD on 08/10/20 1340 Status: Active

Ordering user: Khalily, Cyna, MD 08/10/20 1340 Ordering provider: Khalily, Cyna, MD Authorized by: Khalily, Cyna, MD Ordering mode: Standard Frequency: Routine 08/10/20 - Class: Clinic Performed

Quantity: 1

Diagnoses

Status post arthroscopy of left shoulder [298.890] Order comments: SHOULDER * Regular diet at home * Leave dressing on and clean for 3 days. * May shower when dressing removed if there is no drainage from the incisions. If there _is drainage, keep incision clean and covered. * Use ice or polar pack on and off for the next 2 - 3 days, no more than 20 minutes ata time. * Continue to use sling AT ALL TIMES. You may CAREFULLY remove to shower ONLY. * If you have a nerve block, it will wear off sometime today or tonight. Make sure to start taking pain medicine BEFORE the pain starts. * Call off for shortness of breath and signs of infection. * If you have pictures from surgery, bring them to your follow-up appointment.

Indications

Status post arthroscopy of left shoulder [Z98.890 (ICD-10-CM)]

Title: PreOp/PostOp General Surgery () Topic: Medications - PostOp General Surgery (Resolved)

Point: Antiemetics (Resolved) Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on name, reason, dose, route, and frequency for taking the medication; special instructions and precautions for preparing, self-administering, using, or disposing the medications in the hospital or home; any monitoring that will be done to evaluate the medication and verify its effectiveness; significant side effects including drug-drug, drug-food interactions or therapeutic contraindications that may be encountered and how to avoid and respond to such factors; action to be taken in the event or a wrong or missed dose; next dose due. Give medication specific teaching material.

Point: Post-Op Pneumonia Prevention (Resolved)

Description:

Explain to patient/family/caregiver that people are at risk for pneumonia when they lay around more than usual. Lack of exercise and mobility also increases the risk for pneumonia. Instruct the patient to walk every 2 hours for short distances (if unable to walk, they should sit on bedside every 2 hours and dangle legs unless contraindicated).

Topic: Disease Management - PostOp General Surgery (Resolved)

Point: Edema Management (Resolved) Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver that patient may get puffy and look swollen. Need to reposition frequently, elevate extremities above heart level if possible, use ice packs as ordered, maintain fluid and diet control.

Point: Special Care Needs Related to Specific Surgery (Specify in Comments or Add Teaching Point) (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on the specific care needs, reason, how to perform and expected length of time needed due to surgery.

Point: Incision/Wound Care (Resolved)

Description: Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for incision/wound to include: how to clean, when to clean and any special instruction for cleaning. Know how to care for your wound. Emphasize good hand hygiene before and after caring for the the wound. Teach about signs and symptoms of infection and when to notify healthcare provider. Teach to tell family and friends to never touch wound or bandage and to clean their hands before and after visiting. Instruct to keep incision/wound as clean and dry as possible. Caution against: scrubbing or rubbing the incision, removing the tape strips, using lotion or powder around the incision, exposing the incision to sunlight, taking a bath unless the incision can be kept dry.

Point: Bandage/Dressing Care (Resolved)

Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver on how to care for bandage/dressing to include: how to change, when to change and any special instruction for changing. Demonstrate changing a bandage/dressing and receive a return demonstation. Teach to keep bandage/dressing clean and dry as possible. Caution against: using lots of tape, using lotion or powder around bandage, getting bandage/dressing wet when taking a bath or shower. Emphasize good hand hygiene. If patient is not to change bandage/dressing, instruct him/her to make follow-up appointment with healthcare provider to have bandage/dressing changed.

Encourage the patient/family/caregiver to ask any questions about bandage/dressing care.

Description:

Educate patient/family/caregiver on discharge instructions. Review the physician's discharge instructions with patient and primary caregiver including follow-up appointments, diet, activity, medications and special precautions. If home care services

will be provided, review the role of the home care nurse and

when to expect the first visit.

Provide written instructions as indicated.

Explain to family that they should prepare the home environment for safety/falls prevention if indicated (minimize trip hazards,

keep areas well lit, avoid slippery, wet floors, etc).

Use caution near pets (pets can be a tripping hazard).

If the patient has an incision instruct them to not allow pets to get near the incision so as to decrease the chance of infection.

Instructions

Activity instructions May shower when dressing removed

Diet instructions Advance to home diet as tolerated

Other instructions

Call doctor for: potential signs of infection Any changes to appearance of the site, including foul smelling drainage, increased redness, swelling, tenderness or odor

Call doctor for: difficulty breathing or shortness of breath

Provide home instruction sheet (specify) SHOULDER

* Regular diet at home * Leave dressing on and clean for 3 days. * May shower when dressing removed if there is no drainage from the incisions. If there

is drainage, keep incision clean and covered. * Use ice or polar pack on and off for the next 2 - 3 days, no more than 20 minutes at a

time. * Continue to use sling AT ALL TIMES. You may CAREFULLY remove to

shower ONLY. * If you have a nerve block, it will wear off sometime today or tonight. Make sure to start

taking pain medicine BEFORE the pain starts. * Call off for shortness of breath and signs of infection. * If you have pictures from surgery, bring them to your follow-up appointment.

Remove dressing

Medication Information

Preparation for Your Discharge: Your providers may have already given you your prescription for pain medication prior to your discharge date. If this is the case, it is best to be prepared and have your medication filled and ready for your return home from the hospital. You may want to have your pain pills with you on your ride home if you are traveling a long distance. While at Home: * Use medication only as directed. + Remember medication takes time to work. Most oral pain relievers need at least 20 minutes to take effect. * Remember to take your pain medication before activity and at bedtime. Your provider may advise you to take your pain medication at regular intervals (such as every 4 to 6 hours). * Be sure to get enough rest. If you are having trouble sleeping, talk to your provider. * Use pillows to support you when you sleep and when you do your coughing and deep breathing exercises Try using the alternative methods such as heating pads or cold therapy, walking, guided imagery tapes, listening to soft music, changing your position in bed, aromatherapy, and massage can help relieve your pain.

.

Safe medication storage and disposal Medications should be stored by patients in secure locations and excess pills should be disposed of properly, to minimize the risk of diversion and inappropriate use. To find a prescription drug disposal location near you please

visit https://odcp.ky.gov

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Norton Care Call

If you are going home after leaving the hospital you will receive a Norton Care Call

* Within 48 hours of discharge, you should receive an automated phone call from [PHONE-6220] (Caller ID will read Norton Care Call)

* You will be asked a few questions about your health and progress. Please answer the questions using the phone keypad.

* Based on your answers, a Norton Healthcare team member may call you to offer help and other instructions.

Instructions PATIENT INSTRUCTIONS POST-ANESTHESIA

IMMEDIATELY FOLLOWING SURGERY: Do not drive, drink any alcohol, operate machinery, make important decisions, or sign any legal paperwork for the next twenty four hours after surgery or while taking narcotic pain medications or sedatives. You may experience a sore throat, mild nausea, dizziness, drowsiness, or a headache. These symptoms are normal after surgery and will subside in the next 24 hours. If you develop severe nausea and vomiting, a severe headache, or a high fever please notify your doctor immediately.

DO NOT TAKE TYLENOL WITH YOUR PERCOCET, DRINK PLENTY OF WATER, AND TAKE WITH FOOD.

CALL DR.KHALILY'S OFFICE IF YOU NOTICE ANY SIGNS OR SYMPTOMS OF INFECTION SUCH AS: INCREASED REDNESS, SWELLING, FOUL SMELLING DRAINAGE, FEVER, RED STREAKS, OR CHILLS.

ANESTHESIA DISCLOSURE AND COnaezo co

nt oe “Khalil. Cyne (Provided in Connection with Surgical, Medical or Diagnostic 1 ek a ee services

TO THE PATIENT: Anesthesia is necessary to facilitate the recommended surgicin, meatcal or diagnostic procedure(s) you have consented to undergo and the use of invasive monitors (which are devices that enter the body to aid the physician in making decisions regarding a patient’s vital functions) may be necessary to assist with the provision of the anesthesia. You have the right, as a patient, to be informed about the provision of anesthesia and invasive monitors in connection with the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s) to be used to diagnose or treat your condition.

{ understand that, in connection with the surgical, medical, or diagnostic procedure(s) used to diagnose or treat ‘ry condition, the physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists of Professional Anesthesia Services of Kentucky, PLLC, (my “Anesthesia Provider(s)”) and such associates, technical assistants and other health care providers as they may deem necessary, will be the providers of the anesthesia services and invasive monitors, if necessary.

\na physician who is not affiliated with Professional Anesthesia Services of Kentucky, PLLC (my “Treating Physician”), has alread explained to me what my condition is, and | have already consented to the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s) recommended by my Treating Physician to diagnose or treat my condition.

' | understand that my Treating Physician and/or Anesthesia Provider may discover other or different conditions which require additional or different procedures than those planned. | understand that the type of anesthesia service may have to be changed, possibly without explanation to me. | authorize the Anesthesia Providers to provide such other anesthesia services which are advisable in their professional judgment.

RISKS AND HAZARDS ASSOCIATED WITH ANESTHESIA:

| understand that all forms of anesthesia involve some risks and no guarantees or promises can be made concerning the results of my procedure or treatment. Just as there may be risks and hazards in continuing my present condition without treatment, there are also risks and hazards related to the performance of the surgical, medical or diagnostic procedure(s), including the anesthesia planned for me. | realize that severe complications with anesthesia can occur, including the potential for infection, blood clots in veins and lungs, hemorrhage, drug reactions, respiratory problems, cardiac/cardiovascular/other major organ problems, loss of limb function or sensation, paralysis, stroke, brain damage and even death. | further understand that, in addition to the complications noted above, the following risks and hazards may occur in connection with the forms of anesthesia listed below:

GENERAL ANESTHESIA Description: Total unconscious state, possible placement of a tube into the windpipe Technique: Drug injected into the bloodstream, breathed into the lungs, or administered by other routes Risks: Permanent organ damage; memory dysfunction/memory loss; minor discomfort; mouth, jaw or throat pain; hoarseness; injury to mouth, teeth, lips, gums, vocal cords, or eyes; need for post-operative ventilation; awareness under

anesthesia; and brain damage.

0 EPIDURAL / SPINAL Description: Temporary decrease or loss of feeling and/or movement to lower part of body Technique: Drug injected through a needle/catheter placed either directly into the spinal canal! or immediately outside the spinal canal Risks: Headache; chronic pain; persistent back pain; bleeding/epidural hematoma; infection; nerve injury or damage; “total spinal”; potential to convert to a general anesthetic if the block fails or the procedure outlasts the block; and/or brain damage.

co MONITORED ANESTHESIA CARE WITH SEDATION (MAC or Conscious Sedation) Description: Reduced anxiety and pain, partial or total amnesia Technique: Drug injected into the bloodstream, breathed into the lungs, or administered by other routes producing a semi- conscious state. This may include the administration of drugs to help alleviate anxiety, either alone or as a supplement to a local or regional technique. Risks: Permanent organ damage; brain damage; memory dysfunction/memory loss; potential to convert to generalanesthetic if the sedation is not adequate; an unconscious state; and/or difficulty with breathing. Please know that MAC is a vigilant monitoring of a patient’s vital functions while undergoing therapeutic or diagnostic procedures.

o/MAJOR / MINOR REGIONAL NERVE BLOCK ANESTHESH Right Left. Ieterscalepd Supraclavicular Infraclavicular Axillary Wrist Digital

Femoral Saphenous Popliteal Sciatic Ankle Eye Description: Temporary loss of feeling and/or movement of a limb Technique: Local anesthetic injected via a needle with nerve twitch monitor and/ or ultrasound guidance providing loss of sensation to the limb or area of the operation Risks: Infection; convulsions; weakness; persistent numbness; residual pain; injury to blood vessels (eye block-loss of vision); inability to breathe on your own which may require breathing assistance up to and including placement of a tube into the windpipe; collapsed lung; hematoma; temporary paralysis of the diaphragm; chronic pain; and medical necessity to convert to general anesthesia; nerve damage

RISKS AND HAZARDS ASSOCIATED WITH INVASIVE MONITORS:

Invasive monitors are devices that enter the body to aid the physician in making decisions regarding the patient's vital functions that cannot be obtained by non-invasive monitors. Certain risks involved in using invasive monitors include: injuries to blood vessels and/or the heart; infection; puncture of lung; collapse of the lung; injury to nerves; stroke; loss of digits; anxiety and/or discomfort. ,

ADDITIONAL RISKS TO SPECIFIC SURGERIES:

While complications are rare, different surgeries carry special risks. For example, spinal surgery has specific risks which you and your surgeon may have already discussed, such as the risk of blindness after surgery. Blindness can affect one or both eyes, may be partial or complete, temporary or rarely permanent. This risk is related to prone (belly down) position, as well as multiple other factors. Some risks are not necessarily dependent on the surgery or anesthesia alone. Your age, weight, and other health conditions may also affect your risk. Please do not hesitate to ask your anesthesiologist any questions you may have regarding specific risks to your surgery. Additional risks discussed:

GENERAL CONSENT:

Post-op Visit ACDF C3-4 with plate Date of surgery 1-20-2020

History of Present Illness: Pt is doing pretty well since surgery. She still has some slight swallowing issues but seems to be improving

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days. Norton eCare Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.

Louisville KY 40218-1929 [DOB-6407] - Office Visit in Norton Spine Specialists (continued) Clinical Notes (continued anterior cervical discectomy and fusion with plate secondary to cervical stenosis. She states that she has been doing very well post-operatively. She notes that her pre-operative neck pain has completely resolved. She does

complain of some right shoulder pain, but this is unrelated to her cervical surgery. She denies any pain, numbness, or weakness involving the upper extremities. She is satisfied with the overall surgical outcome.

The following portions of the patient's history were reviewed and updated as appropriate: allergies, current medications, past family history, past medical history, past social history, past surgical history and problem list.

Review of Systems

Constitutional: Negative for activity change, appetite change, fever and unexpected weight change. HENT: Negative for tinnitus, trouble swallowing and voice change.

Respiratory: Negative for shortness of breath and wheezing.

Cardiovascular: Negative for chest pain and palpitations.

Gastrointestinal: Negative for constipation, diarrhea, nausea and vomiting.

Genitourinary: Negative for difficulty urinating, dysuria, frequency and urgency.

Musculoskeletal: Positive for arthralgias. Negative for back pain, gait problem, myalgias, neck pain and neck stiffness.

Skin: Negative for rash and wound.

Allergic/Immunologic: Negative for environmental allergies, food allergies and immunocompromised state. Neurological: Negative for dizziness, weakness, numbness and headaches.

Hematological: Negative for adenopathy. Does not bruise/bleed easily.

All other systems reviewed and are negative.

Objective: Objective Ht 5' 3" (160 cm) | Wt 78 kg (172 Ib) | BMI 30.47 kg/m?

Physical Exam

Constitutional: She is oriented to person, place, and time. She appears well-developed and well-nourished. No distress.

HENT:

Head: Normocephalic and atraumatic.

Eyes: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.

Neck: Normal range of motion.

Pulmonary/Chest: Effort normal. No respiratory distress.

Musculoskeletal:

Comments: On physical examination, she is oriented x3 and is a very good historian. She is an independent ambulator with a normal gait. She has a fairly normal appearing cervical lordosis. She is neurologically intact in both upper extremities with respect sensation and motor. Skin integuments are normal and there are no palpable masses. Extraocular movements are full, facial expressions are symmetrical, respirations are not labored.

Neurological: She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time.

Skin: Skin is warm and dry. She is not diaphoretic.

Psychiatric: She has a normal mood and affect. Her behavior is normal. Vitals reviewed.

Assessment and Plan: Assessment Amy was seen today for neck pain.

Diagnoses and all orders for this visit: Cervical stenosis of spinal canal

S/P cervical spinal fusion

| have spent 25 minutes in consultation with the patient today. | have also spent time extensively reviewing imaging with the patient. Her x-ray showed the instrumentation at C3-4 to be in good position. Based on her symptoms and radiology, | believe that the patient is doing very well post-operatively. She does not report any significant neck pain at this time. In regards to her shoulder pain, | have advised the patient to be seen by a general orthopaedic surgeon for further evaluation. We have discussed the do's and don'ts and | have answered all of her questions. The patient will follow up in 3 months.

Return in about 3 months (around 8/26/2020).

Results Callback

If you had labs or diagnostic tests ordered and you do not receive a phone call, letter or MyChart message regarding your results within 2 weeks, please contact our office to receive your results.

Work Return/Restrictions No documentation.

Injection Instructions

If you received an injection, a small reaction is not uncommon. A low grade fever (up to 100.5°F) and some pain is also not uncommon during the first 24 hours, but unusual beyond. Call the office for a fever of 101.0°F or higher at any time. Any expanding redness or persistent drainage from your injection site should be brought to the attention of your doctor

After-Hours Instructions

For after-hours care, please call the office number and you will be routed to an on-call provider.

MEDICAL RECORDS

To request a copy of your medical records, email the Use and Disclosure Department through your MyNortonChart account by using the Ask Customer Service feature, or

complete a Norton Healthcare authorization form at https://medicalrecordrequest.nortonhealthcare.org

Once received, your request will be processed within 7 business days. Norton eCare Skip the waiting room for your non urgent health issue using Norton eCare through MyNortonChart.

Norton eCare providers are available 24 hours a day, seven days a week through two secure online options using your MyNortonChart account: a video visit or an eVisit.

For additional information on conditions treated and how to schedule an eCare appointment, visit NortonHealthcare.com/ eCare.

Third Party Apps and Epic

Patients of providers that use Epic software are able to configure 3rd party apps to retrieve parts of their health record for their own personal use, including lab results, allergies, medications, and immunization history.

For instructions, visit: https://open.epic.com/Tutorial/PatientAuthentication.

Dear Ms. Mills:

Thank you for visiting our office today. We understand sometimes you leave the office and then think of non-urgent questions you wish you had asked. Well, we’re here for you. Feel free to use the attached questionnaire to ask those questions via MyNortonChart. A staff member or provider will be happy to answer any non-urgent questions you may have.

Your feedback is very important to us! You may be receiving a survey through e-mail and we hope you will take the time to complete it. We value what you have to say and use your feedback to improve our patient care services here at Norton Healthcare. Thank you!

If this is a medical emergency, please call 911 or go to the nearest Norton Healthcare emergency department.

We are privileged to be your choice for medical care. Wishing you good health.
